skill.name: azure-identity-rust
skill.description:
Azure Identity SDK for Rust authentication. Use for DeveloperToolsCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, ClientSecretCredential, and token-based authentication.
Triggers: "azure-identity", "DeveloperToolsCredential", "authentication rust", "managed identity rust", "credential rust".
skill.summary: Azure Identity SDK for Rust authentication. Use for DeveloperToolsCredential, ManagedIdentityCredent...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, network URLs, environment variables, and a critical combined-risk heuristic. Review found no executable scripts or malicious behavior; the evidence is documentation for Azure Identity SDK usage. The skill should be publishable with low risk because it discusses credential configuration and links to external Azure and Rust package resources.

low_findings.title: Credential Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill documents Azure tenant, client, and secret environment variable names. This is expected for an Azure authentication guide, but users should avoid pasting real secrets into prompts or shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly lists Azure credential variables and a client secret placeholder. The examples are documentation-only and do not read, store, or transmit secrets.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples
low_findings.description: The static command findings are Markdown examples such as cargo add and inline Azure CLI references. No executable script, hook, or tool invocation is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious syntax appears inside Markdown code fences or inline formatting. There is no Ruby, shell backtick execution, generated script, or automated command path.

low_findings.title: External Reference Links
low_findings.description: The skill includes an example Key Vault URL and links to docs.rs, GitHub, and crates.io. These are normal reference links, not active network calls or exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references and a placeholder Azure service endpoint. No code in the skill performs network requests.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined-Risk Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: The analyzer combined command, network, and credential patterns into a critical heuristic. Human review found documentation examples only, with no prompt injection text, obfuscation, data exfiltration, or malicious instruction flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All suspicious elements are explained by the skill's stated purpose as an Azure Identity SDK reference. The entire file is Markdown guidance with no hidden executable payload.

content.value_statement: Rust developers need correct Azure authentication patterns without searching across SDK pages. This skill provides focused guidance for Azure Identity credentials, environment setup, and common Rust authentication choices.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to add the azure_identity crate to a Rust project.
Lists Azure environment variables used by service principal authentication.
Summarizes supported credential types in the Azure Identity SDK for Rust.
Shows when to use DeveloperToolsCredential for local development.
Shows when to use ManagedIdentityCredential for Azure-hosted workloads.
Links to official package, API, and source references.
limitations: Does not validate live Azure credentials or tenant configuration.
Does not create Azure resources or assign managed identities.
Does not cover every Azure SDK client or service-specific permission model.
Uses placeholder secrets and IDs that must be replaced securely.

use_cases.title: Local Azure SDK Prototyping
use_cases.description: Set up Rust code that authenticates through local Azure developer tools during early development.

use_cases.title: Production Identity Selection
use_cases.description: Choose between managed identity, service principal secrets, certificates, and workload identity for deployment.

use_cases.title: Credential Review
use_cases.description: Check an existing Rust Azure authentication approach against common SDK credential patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Credential
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a Rust app that connects to Azure. Help me choose the right azure_identity credential for local development and production.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Local Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use DeveloperToolsCredential in a Rust Azure SDK client, and explain the local login requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Managed Identity
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my plan to use ManagedIdentityCredential for an Azure-hosted Rust service. Include system-assigned and user-assigned options.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Credential Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Rust Azure authentication design for secret exposure, credential reuse, and production readiness. Recommend safer Azure Identity patterns.

output_examples.input: Which credential should I use while developing locally?
output_examples.output: Use DeveloperToolsCredential for local development. It can use existing Azure CLI or Azure Developer CLI sign-in state.

output_examples.input: What should I use when the app runs in Azure?
output_examples.output: Use ManagedIdentityCredential when possible. It avoids stored secrets and works with Azure-hosted resources that have an assigned identity.

output_examples.input: Can I use a service principal secret?
output_examples.output: Yes, ClientSecretCredential supports service principal authentication. Store secrets outside source code and avoid sharing them in prompts or logs.

best_practices: Prefer managed identity for production workloads running in Azure.
Use developer tool credentials only for local development workflows.
Keep tenant IDs, client IDs, and secrets out of source code and prompt history.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode client secrets in Rust source files.
Do not use local developer credentials for production deployments.
Do not grant broad Azure permissions when a narrower role is enough.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps users choose and apply Azure Identity SDK credentials in Rust applications.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and credential patterns, but it does not execute commands.

faq.question: Does it require real Azure secrets?
faq.answer: No. The examples use placeholders. Users should keep real secrets outside prompts and source code.

faq.question: Which credential is best for local development?
faq.answer: DeveloperToolsCredential is the recommended local option because it can use Azure CLI or Azure Developer CLI sign-in state.

faq.question: Which credential is best for production in Azure?
faq.answer: ManagedIdentityCredential is usually preferred because it removes the need to manage application secrets.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
